name: FRAGO (Fragmentary Order) / Adaptive Replanning
origin_domain: military (U.S. Army and Joint Forces operations doctrine)
year_developed: 1940s-1950s (codified in FM 5-0 'Planning and Orders Production' and FM 101-5 'Staff Organization and Operations'; continuously refined through subsequent doctrine updates)
progenitor: U.S. Army (Training and Doctrine Command / TRADOC); codified in FM 5-0, FM 6-0, and FM 101-5; the five-paragraph OPORD/FRAGO format traces back to World War II era German Auftragstaktik and U.S. adaptation of mission-type orders
driving_insight: The FRAGO's driving insight is that replanning should be incremental, not holistic — when conditions change, you communicate only what has changed, preserving everything that still holds. This is the doctrinal embodiment of the principle 'no plan survives contact with the enemy' (von Moltke). The FRAGO solves a specific communication and coordination problem: under time pressure and degraded communications, you cannot re-broadcast an entire operations order. A full OPORD might be 20-50 pages; a FRAGO might be a single paragraph or a few bullet points transmitted over radio. By following the same five-paragraph structure as the OPORD but stating 'No change' for unaffected paragraphs, the FRAGO provides a cognitive compression format that recipients can immediately integrate with their existing understanding of the plan. The deeper insight is that this preserves cognitive state — recipients don't have to re-process the entire plan to find what changed; they know exactly what to update. The FRAGO also enables FRAGPLANs — pre-designated branch plans that are fully developed and socialized before execution, then activated by a brief FRAGO when the triggering condition occurs (e.g., 'Execute FRAGPLAN 7'). This shifts replanning from a reactive scramble to a proactive menu of pre-vetted options.
decision_style: hierarchical with mission command delegation. FRAGOs are issued by commanders to subordinates — this is a hierarchical communication channel. However, the content of a FRAGO reflects mission command: it communicates what the commander wants done (intent) and what has changed (the delta), not how to do it. The subordinate retains decision authority over execution methods. The decision to issue a FRAGO is the commander's decision, triggered by reaching a decision point (a pre-identified point in space and time where a key decision must be made). Decision points are planned in advance, and the FRAGO is the vehicle for communicating the decision when the point is reached. This creates a decision architecture where: (1) commanders pre-identify decision points during planning, (2) when conditions trigger a decision point, the commander makes a decision, (3) the FRAGO communicates that decision as a delta to the existing plan. This is fundamentally a deliberative-triggered model: decisions are deliberated in advance (planning phase), triggered by conditions (execution phase), and communicated as deltas (FRAGO).
problem_class_targeted: The FRAGO targets adaptive replanning problems characterized by: (1) plan deviation — conditions have changed from the planning assumptions, requiring plan adaptation; (2) time pressure — there is not enough time to produce and disseminate a full new plan; (3) partial validity — most of the original plan remains valid, and only specific elements need to change; (4) distributed audience — the change must be communicated to multiple subordinate units that already hold the original plan; (5) information asymmetry — the commander issuing the FRAGO has information that subordinates don't, and the FRAGO's job is to transfer that information efficiently; (6) pre-planned contingencies — many adaptations are anticipated through branch plans (FRAGPLANs) that the FRAGO simply activates. The problem class is well-defined: there is an existing plan, something has changed, and the change must be communicated to distributed executors with minimal latency and maximum clarity.
ordered_phases: The FRAGO itself is not a process with phases — it is a communication format and a decision vehicle. The broader adaptive replanning process that the FRAGO serves has implicit phases: (1) MONITOR — continuously assess whether the current plan remains valid against evolving conditions (METT-TC: Mission, Enemy, Terrain/Weather, Troops available, Time, Civil considerations). (2) DETECT — identify when a decision point has been reached or when conditions have deviated from plan assumptions beyond acceptable bounds. (3) DECIDE — the commander makes a decision: execute a pre-planned branch (FRAGPLAN), modify the plan (new FRAGO), or continue with the current plan (no change). (4) FORMULATE — produce the FRAGO using the five-paragraph OPORD format, stating only changes from the original order. (5) DISSEMINATE — transmit the FRAGO to all affected subordinate units through available communications channels. (6) INTEGRATE — subordinates integrate the FRAGO's changes into their understanding of the plan and continue execution. This is not a formal doctrinal phase structure but an implicit pattern of adaptive replanning. The FRAGO is the product of this process, not the process itself.
feedback_loops: The FRAGO system has explicit feedback loops between the issuing commander and receiving subordinates. (1) Confirmation loop: subordinates acknowledge receipt and confirm understanding of the FRAGO (explicit, designed). (2) Clarification loop: if a subordinate does not understand the FRAGO or identifies a conflict with other orders, they request clarification (explicit). (3) Execution feedback: subordinates report execution status, which feeds into the commander's ongoing assessment of whether the adapted plan is working and whether further FRAGOs are needed (explicit). (4) Cross-subordinate coordination: subordinates share FRAGO-driven changes with adjacent units to maintain lateral coordination, preventing the 'left hand doesn't know what the right hand is doing' problem (designed but often emergent). (5) Higher-headquarters feedback: the issuing commander may need to inform higher headquarters of the FRAGO-driven plan change, depending on the scope (explicit when the change affects higher headquarters' plan assumptions). (6) There is no formal loop from FRAGO back to original plan revision — the FRAGO overlays the original plan without modifying the original OPORD document (the original OPORD + all subsequent FRAGOs together constitute the current plan). This is both a strength (preserves state) and a weakness (plan documentation becomes fragmented across multiple FRAGOs).
gates: The FRAGO process has implicit quality gates rather than formal approval gates. (1) Decision point trigger gate: the commander must confirm that a decision point has been reached and that action is required — not every change in conditions triggers a FRAGO; many changes are absorbed within the existing plan's flexibility. (2) Formulation correctness gate: the FRAGO must correctly identify what has changed and preserve what has not — a poorly formulated FRAGO that accidentally changes something that should have been preserved creates confusion. (3) Dissemination completeness gate: all affected subordinates must receive the FRAGO — a FRAGO that doesn't reach a key unit creates plan divergence where different units are executing different versions of the plan. (4) Subordinate integration gate: subordinates must correctly integrate the FRAGO with the existing plan and with any other FRAGOs they have received — plan version management is an acknowledged challenge. If a gate blocks: if the commander cannot reach a decision (Decision gate), the current plan continues by default. If dissemination fails, alternate communications channels are used or the FRAGO is relayed through intermediate units. If a subordinate cannot integrate the FRAGO, they request clarification. There is no formal blocking mechanism — the system defaults to continue with the current plan if the FRAGO cannot be successfully issued.
exit_criteria: A FRAGO is 'done' when: (1) it has been disseminated to all affected subordinates; (2) subordinates have acknowledged receipt and confirmed understanding; (3) the change communicated by the FRAGO has been executed or integrated into ongoing operations. However, a FRAGO is never truly 'done' in the sense of being closed — it becomes part of the cumulative plan (Original OPORD + FRAGO 1 + FRAGO 2 + ... + FRAGO N = Current Plan). The adaptive replanning cycle is continuous for the duration of the operation; it exits only when the operation is complete. Partial completion states: a FRAGO may be partially executed if conditions change again before full implementation, requiring another FRAGO to further modify the plan. This layering of FRAGOs over the original plan is the intended operational mode, not a failure condition.
decomposition_method: The FRAGO does not decompose problems — it communicates plan changes. However, the adaptive replanning process that the FRAGO serves uses dependency-driven decomposition: when conditions change, the commander identifies which elements of the plan are affected (dependencies on the changed condition) and which are unaffected. The FRAGO communicates only the affected elements, preserving unaffected elements by reference (or by stating 'No change'). This is a delta-based decomposition: the plan is decomposed into changed and unchanged components, and only the changed components are transmitted. The five-paragraph OPORD format provides the decomposition structure: Situation, Mission, Execution, Sustainment, Command and Signal. Each paragraph can independently be marked 'No change' or updated, allowing the FRAGO to selectively update only the paragraphs that require change. This is a value-driven decomposition: the value of retransmitting unchanged information is zero (it wastes time and creates confusion), so only changed information is transmitted.
granularity_guidance: A FRAGO decomposes plan changes down to the level of specific paragraph and sub-paragraph modifications. The minimum granularity is a single changed fact (e.g., 'enemy artillery battery has relocated to grid AB123456; adjust Scheme of Fires accordingly'). The maximum granularity of a single FRAGO is a complete replacement of the mission statement (Paragraph 2) and all execution details (Paragraph 3) — effectively a new plan issued via FRAGO format because time precludes issuing a full OPORD. The stopping condition is pragmatic: include enough detail that subordinates can execute the change without ambiguity, but not more detail than time and communications bandwidth allow. FM 5-0 emphasizes that FRAGOs should be 'clear, concise, include risk management, and quickly executed by subordinates.' The doctrine specifies that 'changes that affect the battle are disseminated within five minutes' — this is the temporal constraint that bounds FRAGO granularity.
boundary_rules: The FRAGO enforces boundaries through: (1) The 'No change' convention — any paragraph not explicitly modified in the FRAGO remains as stated in the original OPORD or the most recent FRAGO for that paragraph. This creates a clear boundary between what has changed and what has not. (2) Paragraph structure boundary — each of the five paragraphs (Situation, Mission, Execution, Sustainment, Command and Signal) has a defined scope, and the FRAGO maintains these boundaries. (3) Authority boundary — only the issuing commander (or a successor in command) can issue a FRAGO modifying their own order; subordinates cannot issue FRAGOs that change the commander's intent, though they can issue their own FRAGOs modifying their own subordinate orders. (4) Temporal boundary — a FRAGO is effective from the time it is issued until superseded by a subsequent FRAGO or a new OPORD; there is no automatic expiration. The boundary between successive FRAGOs is managed through sequential numbering (FRAGO 1, FRAGO 2, etc.) but is a recognized challenge — when does the accumulated set of FRAGOs become so extensive that issuing a consolidated new OPORD is more efficient than issuing another FRAGO?
risk_assessment_mechanism: Risk assessment is integrated into the FRAGO formulation process through the Army's Composite Risk Management (CRM) framework. When the commander decides to issue a FRAGO modifying the plan, they must: (1) identify hazards introduced by the change; (2) assess the probability and severity of each hazard; (3) develop controls to mitigate the risks; (4) make a risk acceptance decision (is the residual risk acceptable given the mission?). The FRAGO must include these risk controls. When a FRAGO activates a FRAGPLAN (pre-planned branch), the risk assessment was conducted during the original planning process, and the FRAGO simply confirms that the risk assessment remains valid. When a FRAGO involves a new, unplanned adaptation, the risk assessment must be conducted in real-time, which introduces the risk of incomplete or rushed risk assessment under time pressure. FM 5-0 emphasizes that risk management in FRAGOs should be explicit: the FRAGO should state what risks are being accepted and what risk controls are in place.
unknown_handling: When the FRAGO process encounters unknowns during adaptive replanning: (1) Intelligence gaps — the commander may need to make a decision with incomplete information; doctrine provides for decisions based on 'best available information' with explicit acknowledgment of uncertainty. (2) Subordinate status unknown — if the commander cannot confirm a subordinate's status (e.g., communications lost), the FRAGO may be issued with instructions for when communications are re-established, or the commander may act on the assumption that the subordinate is executing the last received orders. (3) FRAGPLAN applicability unknown — if it is unclear which FRAGPLAN to activate, the commander uses their judgment based on available information; a wrong FRAGPLAN activation is recoverable through subsequent FRAGOs. (4) Effects of change unknown — the commander may not be able to predict all consequences of the plan change; this is accepted as inherent to adaptive replanning under time pressure. (5) The FRAGO does not have a built-in mechanism for handling unknowns — it relies on the commander's judgment and the principle that imperfect action is better than perfect inaction. The FRAGO is fundamentally a decision-making and communication tool; it assumes a decision has been made and communicates it, rather than helping to make the decision under uncertainty.
default_stance_on_unknown: PERMISSIVE (act on best available information). The FRAGO's doctrinal context is explicitly biased toward action over analysis: 'a good plan executed now is better than a perfect plan executed too late.' When the commander has incomplete information but a decision point has been reached, the default is to decide and issue a FRAGO rather than wait for more information. This permissive stance is counterbalanced by: (1) the pre-planned FRAGPLAN system, which reduces the unknowns by anticipating contingencies; (2) risk acceptance explicit in the FRAGO, which makes the commander accountable for decisions made under uncertainty; (3) the ability to issue subsequent FRAGOs to correct course if the initial adaptation proves wrong. The FRAGO system is fundamentally designed for an environment of high uncertainty and rapid change; a restrictive stance on unknowns would defeat its purpose.
safety_guardrails: In the military context, the FRAGO's safety guardrails are procedural and hierarchical. (1) Commander accountability: the commander issuing the FRAGO is personally accountable for the decision and must explicitly accept the risks. (2) ROE constraints: a FRAGO cannot change Rules of Engagement unless the commander has ROE-change authority; ROE violations through FRAGOs are a doctrinal and legal prohibition. (3) Risk management integration: the CRM framework requires hazard identification, risk assessment, and control implementation before the FRAGO is issued. (4) Subordinate pushback: subordinates have a duty to question a FRAGO that they believe is based on a misunderstanding of the situation or that creates unacceptable risk — this is the 'loyal dissent' principle. (5) Higher headquarters notification: for FRAGOs that significantly change the plan, the issuing commander must inform higher headquarters to maintain strategic alignment. (6) In an AI-agent context, FRAGO-style incremental replanning would need guardrails including: human approval for FRAGOs that change core constraints (analogous to ROE changes); automated validation that the FRAGO is internally consistent and doesn't violate system constraints; an audit log of all FRAGOs preserving the plan's version history; and the ability to roll back to a previous plan version if a FRAGO produces adverse outcomes.
human_decision_points: Humans are IN the loop for every FRAGO decision. The key human decision points: (1) Decision point recognition — a human commander or staff officer recognizes that a decision point has been reached or that conditions have changed; this is a human cognitive act that has not been automated. (2) Decision formulation — the commander personally makes the decision to adapt the plan and determines what the adaptation should be. (3) FRAGO formulation — a human staff officer translates the commander's decision into the five-paragraph FRAGO format. (4) Risk acceptance — the commander personally accepts the risks of the plan change. (5) Dissemination authorization — the commander or authorized staff officer approves the FRAGO for transmission. (6) Subordinate integration — human subordinate commanders interpret the FRAGO and integrate it into their understanding. In the Thunderforge program's 'human-on-the-loop' vision, AI might generate draft FRAGOs based on detected condition changes, but the human commander would review and approve before dissemination. The decision to change the plan is considered too consequential for full automation.
override_capability: Override capability is inherent and continuous. (1) A commander can issue a new FRAGO at any time that overrides a previous FRAGO. (2) A higher commander can override a subordinate commander's FRAGO if it conflicts with higher-level intent. (3) A subordinate can request override (request clarification or express concern) but cannot unilaterally override the commander's FRAGO — they must execute or express their inability to execute (the 'loyal dissent' principle). (4) Override granularity is per-FRAGO: a new FRAGO can reverse all or part of a previous FRAGO. (5) The system is designed for continuous overrides — the cycle of FRAGO → FRAGO → FRAGO is the normal operational mode for a dynamic operation. Overrides are not failures; they are expected adaptations. The only limitation is communications: if communications are lost, the subordinate continues with the last received orders until communications are re-established.
fallback_when_no_human: The FRAGO system has no provision for autonomous operation without human commanders. If the commander is incapacitated or unreachable, command passes to the deputy commander or the next senior officer — another human assumes the FRAGO decision authority. If communications are lost entirely, subordinates continue executing the last received orders (the original OPORD plus any received FRAGOs) until communications are re-established or the situation changes so fundamentally that the subordinate must exercise initiative within the commander's intent (mission command). This is not an 'auto-FRAGO' mechanism — it is a human commander applying judgment to a changing situation. In an AI-agent context, the fallback design would be: if no human is available to approve a replanning event, (a) the agent system continues with the current plan for non-critical adaptations; (b) for critical adaptations (analogous to ROE changes or mission changes), the system halts and waits for human availability; (c) pre-authorized adaptations (analogous to FRAGPLANs) can be auto-executed within pre-established boundaries. The key design principle is that the scope of autonomous adaptation should be explicitly bounded during the planning phase, with unbounded adaptations requiring human approval.
max_problem_size: The FRAGO has been demonstrated at all scales of military operations, from a squad leader issuing a verbal FRAGO to reposition a single fire team, to a corps commander issuing a written FRAGO affecting 100,000+ troops. The 1991 Desert Storm FRAGPLAN 7 (the 'Hail Mary' / 'right hook' maneuver) is the canonical large-scale example: a single FRAGO swung VII Corps (150,000 soldiers) east across ~100 miles of front, requiring changes to artillery targets, resupply points, medical stations, and maneuver control measures. The theoretical limit is constrained by: (1) communications bandwidth — the FRAGO must reach all affected units; (2) cascading effects — a FRAGO at high echelon triggers subordinate FRAGOs at lower echelons, and the cascade time may exceed the available adaptation time; (3) plan complexity — as the number of subordinate units and interdependent tasks increases, the FRAGO's 'what has changed vs. what has not' boundary becomes harder to define cleanly. What breaks at scale: (a) the FRAGO format's conciseness breaks down when too many plan elements need to change simultaneously — at some scale, issuing a new OPORD is more efficient than issuing a FRAGO that effectively rewrites most paragraphs; (b) version management across the distributed force becomes chaotic — different units may be operating on different FRAGO version histories.
parallel_execution: FRAGO execution is naturally parallel: once issued, all subordinate units can integrate the FRAGO and adapt their execution simultaneously. The FRAGO itself is disseminated in parallel (broadcast to all affected units) rather than sequentially. Subordinate FRAGOs (each subordinate unit issuing its own FRAGO to its own subordinates based on the higher-level FRAGO) also execute in parallel — each unit independently adapts its own plans. Dependencies between parallel adaptations are managed through: (1) lateral coordination between adjacent units; (2) the hierarchical structure (higher commander's FRAGO provides the integration framework); (3) battle rhythm synchronization points (periodic updates where all units report status and reconcile plan versions). Parallelism is first-class — the FRAGO system is designed for a distributed force where all units must adapt simultaneously to maintain synchronization. The primary parallelism challenge is not technical but cognitive: ensuring that all subordinate units reach the same interpretation of the FRAGO when they integrate it in parallel without the opportunity for synchronous discussion.
team_scaling_model: The FRAGO system scales linearly with the number of echelons (each echelon adds a FRAGO cascade step) but sub-linearly with the number of units at each echelon (broadcast dissemination enables simultaneous receipt). The limiting factor is the cascade time from highest to lowest echelon: a corps-level FRAGO must be received, processed, and retransmitted as division-level FRAGOs, then brigade-level FRAGOs, then battalion-level FRAGOs. Each cascade step adds latency. The one-third/two-thirds rule of planning applies: higher echelons should use only one-third of the available planning time, leaving two-thirds for subordinate echelons. This creates linear latency scaling with the number of echelons. Large, flat organizations (many units at few echelons) scale better under FRAGO dissemination than tall, hierarchical organizations (few units at many echelons).
cost_scaling_model: The computational/communication cost of a FRAGO scales with message size (roughly proportional to the number of changed plan elements) multiplied by the number of receiving units. FRAGO message size is typically much smaller than a full OPORD — a FRAGO might be a few paragraphs, while a full OPORD might be 20-50 pages. The cognitive cost of FRAGO integration scales linearly with the number of changes (subordinates must update their mental model for each changed element) and with the recency of the original plan (a FRAGO to a plan briefed yesterday is easier to integrate than a FRAGO to a plan briefed a week ago). In an LLM-agent context, FRAGO-style incremental replanning is computationally cheap: instead of regenerating the entire plan (which requires re-running the full LLM planning pipeline), only the changed elements are regenerated. This creates significant cost savings for large plans with small changes — the exact cost profile the FRAGO is designed for. The branching factor is the number of changed plan elements, which is typically small (1-5 major changes per FRAGO).
replanning_built_in: Replanning is not just built into the FRAGO system — the FRAGO IS the replanning mechanism. It is a first-class adaptation vehicle, not an afterthought. (1) Local replanning: most FRAGOs change specific elements of the plan while preserving the overall scheme — this is local compensation. (2) Global replanning: a FRAGO can change the mission statement itself (Paragraph 2), constituting global replanning, but this is less common. (3) Pre-planned replanning: FRAGPLANs are pre-developed branches activated by FRAGOs — this shifts replanning from an execution-time activity to a planning-time activity, reducing execution-time replanning cost. (4) The FRAGO system is designed so that replanning is continuous and expected — operations proceed as a sequence of plan → execute → adapt (FRAGO) → execute → adapt (FRAGO) → ..., not plan → execute → (done or failure). This continuous adaptation model is the doctrinal expression of mission command: the commander adapts the plan as the situation evolves, communicating changes as FRAGOs.
trigger_mechanism: FRAGOs are triggered by: (1) Decision point reached — a pre-identified condition in time and space triggers a pre-planned decision, communicated via FRAGO. (2) Environmental change — enemy actions, weather, terrain, or civil considerations change beyond the plan's assumptions. (3) Mission change — higher headquarters changes the mission, requiring a FRAGO to realign. (4) Asset change — significant friendly capability gain (reinforcements arrive) or loss (unit becomes combat-ineffective). (5) Opportunity detection — an unanticipated enemy vulnerability is identified, and the commander issues a FRAGO to exploit it. (6) Failure detection — a unit fails to achieve its objective, requiring a FRAGO to reconstitute the effort. Triggers are event-driven (condition-based) and time-driven (decision points at specific times), with condition-based triggers being more common in dynamic operations. The FRAGO itself is the response to the trigger, not the trigger detection mechanism — the commander's staff is responsible for monitoring conditions and identifying when triggers have occurred.
adaptation_cost: The FRAGO is explicitly designed to minimize adaptation cost. (1) Communication cost: minimal — the FRAGO transmits only the delta, not the full plan. (2) Cognitive cost for subordinates: low to moderate — subordinates must integrate the delta into their existing mental model of the plan, which is cognitively easier than processing a full new plan. (3) Lost work: minimal to none — the FRAGO preserves everything that still holds; only the work directly affected by the changed elements is lost. (4) State preservation: excellent — the original OPORD and all subsequent FRAGOs together form the complete current plan, and any element not explicitly changed is preserved. (5) Cascade cost: each echelon must translate the higher-level FRAGO into its own subordinate FRAGOs, adding staff work at each echelon. (6) Version management cost: over many FRAGOs, the cognitive overhead of maintaining the plan version history grows; at some point, issuing a consolidated new OPORD becomes cheaper than issuing another FRAGO. The FRAGO is optimal for frequent, small changes; it is sub-optimal for infrequent, large changes where a new OPORD would be more efficient.
failure_recovery_mechanism: The FRAGO system has layered failure recovery. (1) Communication failure — if a FRAGO does not reach a subordinate, the subordinate continues executing the last received orders; when communications are re-established, the subordinate receives all missed FRAGOs and synchronizes. This is mission command's version of eventual consistency. (2) FRAGO misinterpretation — if a subordinate misunderstands a FRAGO, the resulting execution failure is detected through status reporting, and a clarifying FRAGO is issued. (3) FRAGO cascade failure — if an intermediate echelon fails to translate a higher-level FRAGO into subordinate FRAGOs, the plan diverges; recovery requires the higher commander to bypass the failed echelon and issue FRAGOs directly to lower echelons. (4) FRAGO accumulation failure — after many FRAGOs, the plan becomes too fragmented to manage; recovery is to issue a consolidated new OPORD that incorporates all previous FRAGOs. (5) Blast radius containment: a FRAGO that produces a locally bad outcome is contained to the affected units; other units operating under other parts of the plan are unaffected. (6) Rollback: there is no formal FRAGO rollback mechanism — a bad FRAGO is corrected by issuing another FRAGO that reverses the problematic changes. This creates a FRAGO trace that documents the decision history but can become confusing if FRAGOs repeatedly reverse each other.
output_artifacts: The FRAGO produces: (1) The FRAGO itself — a structured document in five-paragraph OPORD format, containing only changes from the original order. (2) FRAGO annexes — maps, overlays, fire support matrices, and other attachments as needed, containing only changed elements. (3) FRAGO acknowledgement log — record of which subordinates acknowledged receipt and understanding. (4) Updated Common Operational Picture (COP) — the COP reflects the plan changes communicated by the FRAGO. (5) Cumulative plan version — the sequence of Original OPORD + FRAGO 1 + FRAGO 2 + ... + FRAGO N, which together constitute the complete current plan. (6) Decision log — record of the commander's decision, the rationale, and the risk acceptance. These artifacts are operational products, not archival products — they exist to support execution, not to document history (though they serve both purposes).
briefing_format: The FRAGO IS the briefing format — it is both the decision record and the communication product. It uses the same five-paragraph OPORD structure (Situation, Mission, Execution, Sustainment, Command and Signal) that all recipients are trained to read and execute. The compression format is 'change-only': each paragraph either states the new information or states 'No change.' This compression is possible because all recipients already have and understand the original OPORD. For verbal FRAGOs (radio, face-to-face), the compression is even tighter: 'FRAGO follows: enemy armor battalion now at Phase Line Blue. Continue mission. Acknowledge.' The FRAGO format is designed for maximum communication efficiency under degraded conditions — it is the military's answer to the question 'how do you communicate a plan change in 30 seconds over a noisy radio to someone who already has the 50-page plan?'
traceability: The FRAGO system provides decent traceability through its sequential structure, but it is not as rigorous as the JTC's. (1) Each FRAGO is sequentially numbered (FRAGO 1, FRAGO 2, etc.) and time-stamped, creating an ordered history of plan changes. (2) The cumulative plan (Original OPORD + all FRAGOs) is the traceable record of every plan decision. (3) The decision log captures the commander's rationale for each FRAGO decision. (4) However, traceability degrades as FRAGOs accumulate: after 20+ FRAGOs, reconstructing the current plan from the FRAGO sequence becomes labor-intensive, and it's easy to miss a FRAGO that modifies a sub-element of a paragraph that was previously modified by an earlier FRAGO. (5) There is no automated plan version management — it relies on human staff officers to maintain the cumulative plan. (6) In an AI-agent context, FRAGO-style incremental plan updates could achieve git-like traceability: each FRAGO is a commit, the plan is a file tree, and the current plan is the HEAD of the FRAGO branch. This is a natural and powerful mapping.
agent_communication_protocol: In the military context, FRAGOs are transmitted via all available communications channels: radio (voice), digital messaging (JADOCS, AFATDS, chat), physical delivery (runner), and face-to-face briefings. These are military-specific or general-purpose channels, not a dedicated protocol. In an AI-agent context, FRAGO-style incremental plan updates would use: (1) a structured message format specifying the plan version being modified (plan ID + plan version), the changed elements, and the new values; (2) versioned plan storage (the plan as a versioned data structure, akin to a git repository); (3) broadcast or publish-subscribe for dissemination (all agents subscribe to plan updates and receive FRAGOs automatically); (4) acknowledgement protocol (agents acknowledge receipt and confirm they have successfully integrated the FRAGO or flag conflicts). MCP or A2A would be the natural transport layer, with the FRAGO format defined as a structured message type within the protocol. The key requirement is that all agents share a common plan representation and can apply delta updates to it.
learning_memory_architecture: The FRAGO system is fundamentally an operational adaptation mechanism, not a learning mechanism. It does not have a built-in learning architecture. However, it integrates with the broader military learning system: (1) After Action Reviews analyze the FRAGO decision history to identify whether the right FRAGOs were issued at the right times, feeding lessons into future planning. (2) The Center for Army Lessons Learned (CALL) archives FRAGOs from significant operations as historical records. (3) The FRAGPLAN system is a form of institutional memory: pre-developed branches capture organizational learning about common contingencies. (4) The FRAGO decision log provides the raw material for studying commander decision-making under uncertainty. (5) In an AI-agent context, FRAGO history would be a rich source of training data for learning when and how to adapt plans — a corpus of 'condition → adaptation' pairs that could train an adaptive replanning model or inform retrieval-augmented generation for the replanning agent.
latency_bounds: FRAGOs operate under near-real-time constraints. Doctrine specifies that 'changes that affect the battle are disseminated within five minutes' — this is an aspirational latency target for the FRAGO formulation and dissemination process. For pre-planned FRAGPLANs, the latency is essentially instantaneous — the commander issues a brief execute order ('Execute FRAGPLAN 7') and all units immediately know what to do. For unplanned FRAGOs, latency includes: (1) condition detection time (how long it takes to recognize that a decision point has been reached); (2) decision time (the commander's decision cycle); (3) formulation time (translating the decision into a properly formatted FRAGO); (4) dissemination time (transmitting the FRAGO to all affected units); (5) integration time (subordinates incorporating the FRAGO into their operations). Degradation under time pressure: as available time compresses, the FRAGO becomes less formal (verbal rather than written), the staff process is shortened, and risk assessment is abbreviated. The five-minute target is realistic for pre-briefed FRAGPLANs but aspirational for novel adaptations.
evaluation_benchmarking: No standardized quantitative benchmarks exist for FRAGO effectiveness. Effectiveness is evaluated qualitatively: (1) Did the FRAGO reach all affected units in time? (2) Did subordinates correctly interpret and execute the FRAGO? (3) Did the FRAGO achieve the intended adaptation (did it fix the problem it was trying to fix)? (4) Did the FRAGO create unintended consequences (did it break something else)? (5) Would a different FRAGO (or no FRAGO) have produced a better outcome? These questions are evaluated through After Action Reviews and operational analysis, not through standardized benchmarks. In an LLM-agent context, FRAGO-style incremental replanning could be benchmarked by: (a) adaptation latency — time from condition change to plan update; (b) adaptation accuracy — does the updated plan correctly address the changed condition without introducing errors; (c) delta conciseness — how much smaller is the FRAGO than a full replan; (d) version coherence — do all agents converge to the same plan version after applying the FRAGO.
mapping_strength: The FRAGO concept maps exceptionally well to LLM-agent plan adaptation. It directly addresses one of the hardest problems in agent systems: how to update a plan that has already been distributed to multiple agents without re-distributing the entire plan and without creating version divergence. Specific strengths: (1) The 'change-only' delta format maps directly to efficient plan updates — instead of regenerating the entire plan JSON, only the changed nodes are updated. (2) The FRAGPLAN concept maps to pre-computed contingency plans — the planning agent generates not just the primary plan but also branch plans for likely failure modes, and the orchestrator activates them with a brief trigger when conditions are met. (3) The five-paragraph structure maps to a structured plan schema with defined sections (objectives, tasks, resource allocations, communication plan, constraints). (4) The 'No change' convention maps to null/unchanged fields in plan data — agents receiving a plan update can rely on any field not explicitly updated remaining valid. (5) The acknowledgment protocol maps to distributed consensus — all agents acknowledge receipt of the plan update, confirming that the distributed system has achieved a consistent state. (6) The version numbering (FRAGO 1, FRAGO 2, ...) maps to plan versioning, enabling agents to detect if they have missed an update (I'm on version 3, you're on version 5 — what did I miss?). (7) The mission command philosophy maps to agent autonomy: the plan update specifies what changed and what to achieve, not how to achieve it — the agent retains execution autonomy within the updated constraints.
gaps: Several aspects of the FRAGO concept need translation for agent systems. (1) The FRAGO assumes human cognitive integration — subordinates 'understand' the plan and can integrate deltas using common sense; agents need explicit, machine-readable plan representations and deterministic delta application rules. (2) The FRAGO assumes a hierarchical command structure; agent systems may have peer-to-peer coordination without a single 'commander' issuing FRAGOs. (3) The FRAGO has no formal conflict resolution — if two FRAGOs modify the same plan element, human subordinates use judgment to resolve; agents need explicit conflict resolution rules. (4) The FRAGO has no formal consistency verification — there is no automated check that the FRAGO is consistent with the original plan and with other FRAGOs; humans do this check intuitively. (5) The FRAGO 'No change' convention relies on shared understanding of what the current plan is; in agent systems, this requires all agents to maintain a synchronized plan state, which is a distributed systems problem. (6) The FRAGO does not specify granularity: when is a change 'big enough' to warrant a FRAGO vs. being absorbed within existing plan flexibility? In agent systems, this threshold would need to be explicitly defined. (7) The FRAGO is designed for broadcast communication (one-to-many); agent systems may need more complex communication patterns (peer-to-peer delegation, multi-party negotiation).
adaptation_required: To apply the FRAGO concept to the Jordan pipeline: (1) Implement a versioned plan data structure where the current plan is a tree of nodes, each with a version identifier, and the FRAGO is a list of node updates (node ID, old version, new value, new version). (2) Define a FRAGO message format as a structured data type in the inter-agent protocol. (3) The Jordan replan node should generate FRAGO-style incremental plan updates rather than full plan regenerations — local compensation is the default; full replan is the escalation. (4) Implement FRAGPLAN support: during the plan phase, the planning agent generates not only the primary plan but also contingency branches for the top-N identified risks, each tagged with a trigger condition. During execution, the orchestrator monitors for trigger conditions and auto-activates FRAGPLANs without re-invoking the full planning pipeline. (5) Implement plan version tracking: every agent maintains its current plan version, and the orchestrator maintains the authoritative plan. When an agent requests a FRAGO, the orchestrator only sends the deltas since the agent's reported version. (6) Implement plan consistency verification: after applying a FRAGO, agents run a lightweight consistency check (does the updated plan still satisfy all constraints? are there dependency conflicts introduced by the change?). (7) Human approval for 'strategic FRAGOs': FRAGOs that change the overall objectives or constraints (analogous to Paragraph 2 Mission or ROE changes) require human approval in Jordan's approval_gate node; FRAGOs that adjust tactical execution within existing constraints can be auto-applied.
analogous_jordan_node: replan (primary), approval_gate (secondary). The FRAGO is the replan node's output format — it is the vehicle for communicating incremental plan adaptations. The decision to issue a FRAGO that changes fundamental constraints maps to the approval_gate.
validation_context: The FRAGO has been validated through: (1) over 80 years of military operations from World War II through current conflicts; (2) every major U.S. military campaign (Korea, Vietnam, Desert Storm, Iraq, Afghanistan) has involved extensive FRAGO usage; (3) the Desert Storm 'Hail Mary' / FRAGPLAN 7 maneuver (1991) is the canonical example of large-scale FRAGO-enabled adaptation; (4) continuous refinement through Army doctrine (FM 5-0, FM 6-0) and lessons learned; (5) adaptation by other services (U.S. Navy CNO FRAGOs, U.S. Marine Corps tactical FRAGOs). The FRAGO is one of the most battle-tested communication formats in existence, validated across every scale of conflict and every operational domain. The fact that it has remained essentially unchanged in its five-paragraph structure for over 50 years is strong evidence of its effectiveness.
known_failures: Documented failure modes: (1) FRAGO not received — a unit does not receive a critical FRAGO and continues executing the old plan, leading to plan divergence; a common failure in degraded communications environments. (2) FRAGO misinterpretation — a unit receives the FRAGO but misunderstands what has changed, applying the delta to the wrong plan element or drawing incorrect inferences about what to change. (3) FRAGO cascade failure — a FRAGO at one echelon is not translated into subordinate FRAGOs at lower echelons, breaking the adaptation chain. (4) FRAGO accumulation — after many FRAGOs, the cumulative plan becomes too complex for human staff officers to manage, and errors in plan version management accumulate. (5) FRAGO contradiction — two FRAGOs modify the same plan element in conflicting ways, and subordinates must resolve the conflict without guidance. (6) FRAGO overcorrection — a FRAGO responds to a condition change but over-adjusts, creating new problems that require further FRAGOs, potentially leading to an oscillating cycle of overcorrections. (7) Inappropriate FRAGO — a commander issues a FRAGO when conditions do not warrant a plan change, creating unnecessary disruption and plan fragmentation. (8) Missed FRAGO — a commander fails to issue a FRAGO when conditions have changed and adaptation is needed, either because the condition change was not detected or because the commander hesitated. (9) Communications-dependent failure — the FRAGO system requires functioning communications; when communications are systematically degraded (electronic warfare, cyber attack), the entire adaptive replanning capability collapses.
where_still_used: Active use: (1) All U.S. Army units — FRAGOs are a core component of the Operations Process (ADP 5-0) and are used continuously during operations. (2) All U.S. service branches — each service has its own FRAGO-equivalent format. (3) U.S. Navy — CNO FRAGOs for strategic guidance modifications. (4) U.S. Marine Corps — FRAGOs taught at The Basic School as a core tactical leadership tool. (5) NATO forces — interoperable FRAGO/FRAGORD procedures. (6) Joint operations — FRAGORDs for multinational mission partner adaptation. (7) The FRAGO format is taught at every level of military education, from Officer Candidate School through the War College. (8) Agile/DevOps retrospectives and sprint adjustments are a civilian descendant of the FRAGO concept — identifying what to change and communicating only the change to the team.
